content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

January 3, 1997


Pamela L. Wilson
Sullivan, Wertz, McDade & Wallace
945 Fourth Avenue
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. I-96-306

Dear Ms. Wilson:

This is in response to your request for informal advice¹ under the lobbyist employer
reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act.²

QUESTIONS

1. Must a lobbyist employer report as "other payments to influence" that portion of a
payment to a public relations consultant for drafting a letter to a member of the Legislature to be
signed by the employer's chief executive officer?

2. If yes, how does the lobbyist employer determine how much of the total payment to
the public relations consultant is attributable to the time spent to draft the letter?

3. For purposes of the lobbyist "contacts" test, is the contact attributable to the consultant
drafting the letter, or to the employer's chief executive officer who signs the letter?


¹ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

² Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California
Code of Regulations, Section 18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-96-306
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes, payments to a public relations consultant to draft a letter to a member of the State
Legislature must be reported by the lobbyist employer.

2. Since the responsibility to report this payment rests with the lobbyist employer, the
lobbyist employer must make a good faith effort to determine how much of the total payment to
the public relations consultant is attributed to drafting the letter.

3. The chief executive officer who is communicating with the legislative member, not the
consultant drafting the letter, will be the person making the lobbyist "contact."

FACTS

You have general questions regarding how to attribute payments made to a public
relations consultant. Basically, if a lobbyist employer contracts with a public relations consultant
for general non-lobbying services, but assigns a specific task related to lobbying activities, such
as a drafting a letter to be sent to a member of the Legislature, the lobbyist employer would have to
report the amount of that payment as "other payments to influence." However, if the public
relations consultant is not a lobbyist, it has no obligation to maintain records of payments it
receives, and has no obligation to allocate that portion of the total payment which was used to
draft the letter. You are asking how a lobbyist employer would know how much of a total
payment is attributed to a specific task which would be reportable as an "other payment to
influence."

Additionally, under the lobbyist "contacts" test, a letter to a member of the Legislature
regarding a specific piece of legislation is a contact which is used to determine when an
individual becomes a lobbyist. You are concerned that the public relations consultant who drafts
the letter would be attributed the contact.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires lobbyist employers³ to file periodic reports disclosing such things as
payments to lobbying firms, payments to lobbyists employed by the employer, a description of

³ A lobbyist employer means any person, other than a lobbying firm, who employs one
or more lobbyists for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel
expenses, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, or contracts for the
services of a lobbying firm for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable
travel expense, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action. (Section
82039.5.)

Advice File No. I-96-306
Page 3

lobbying interests, activity expenses, and the total of all other payments made by the employer to
influence legislative or administrative action. (Section 86116.) "Other payments to influence
legislative or administrative action" include payments for, or in connection with, direct
communication with a legislative, agency, or elective state official for the primary purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action. (Regulation 18616(a)(4)(B).)

Accordingly, when a lobbyist employer makes a payment to a consultant for the purpose
of drafting a letter to a member of the Legislature, the payment must be reported by the lobbyist
employer as an "other payment to influence." When such payment is made to a lobbyist or
lobbying firm which also conducts non-lobbying activities for the employer, it is easy to
determine how much the employer paid for such activity. Lobbyists and lobbying firms are
required to maintain a cash receipts journal showing a record of all payments received in
connection with lobbying activity. (Regulation 18612(a).)

As you pointed out in your letter, the requirement to maintain records does not apply to a
public relations firm which is not a lobbyist or lobbying firm, but nevertheless has accomplished
a specific task which would require its client, a lobbyist employer, to report the payment as
"other payments to influence."

The requirement to report payments made for its lobbying activities rests with the
lobbyist employer. It is the employer's responsibility to ascertain the necessary information
from the public relations consultant. If unable to do so, the employer must make a good faith
effort to determine how much of the total payment to the public relations consultant is attributed
to drafting the letter.

You expressed a further concern that if a public relations consultant drafts a letter to be
sent to a member of the Legislature, the letter could constitute a "contact" for the consultant for
purposes of qualifying as a lobbyist.

A 'lobbyist' is defined as:

Any individual who is employed or contracts for economic consideration,
other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to communicate directly

4 Please be advised that effective January 1, 1997, the definition of a "lobbyist" means
any individual who receives two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more in economic consideration in
a calendar month, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, or whose principal
duties as an employee are, to communicate directly or through his or her agents with any elective
state official, agency official, or legislative official for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action. (Section 82039, amended by Proposition 208 of the November 1996
Statewide General Election. This section is subject to further interpretation by Commission
regulation.)

Advice File No. I-96-306
Page 4

or through his or her agents with any elective state official, agency official or
legislative official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
action, if a substantial or regular portion of the activities for which he or she
receives consideration is for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action. (Section 82039.)

An individual receives consideration for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action when he or she receives or becomes entitled to receive any amount of
compensation for engaging in direct communication, other than administrative testimony, with a
qualifying official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on at least
25 separate occasions in any two consecutive calendar months. (Regulation 18239 (c).)

"Direct communication" as used above means appearing as a witness before, talking to
(either by telephone or in person), corresponding with, or answering questions or inquiries from,
any qualifying official. (Regulation 18239(d)(3).)

In the scenario you provided, the letter to a legislative member is sent under the signature
of the lobbyist employer's chief executive officer. The public relations consultant merely drafts
the letter. It is the chief executive officer, not the consultant, who is directly communicating
with a qualifying official. The lobbyist contacts test would not apply to the public relations
consultant under these circumstances.

I trust this answers your questions sufficiently. If I can be of further assistance, please
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

SGC/JET/jt

sections.conclusion:
1. Yes, payments to a public relations consultant to draft a letter to a member of the State
Legislature must be reported by the lobbyist employer.
2. Since the responsibility to report this payment rests with the lobbyist employer, the
lobbyist employer must make a good faith effort to determine how much ofthe total payment to
the public relations consultant is attributed to drafting the letter.
3. The chief executive officer who is communicating with the legislative member, not the
consultant drafting the letter, will be the person making the lobbyist “contact.”

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes, payments to a public relations consultant to draft a letter to a member of the State
Legislature must be reported by the lobbyist employer.
2. Since the responsibility to report this payment rests with the lobbyist employer, the
lobbyist employer must make a good faith effort to determine how much ofthe total payment to
the public relations consultant is attributed to drafting the letter.
3. The chief executive officer who is communicating with the legislative member, not the
consultant drafting the letter, will be the person making the lobbyist “contact.”
